commentary_block: Prayer of Manasseh is a compact penitential prayer with four movements: invocation of God’s titles and cosmic sovereignty (vv.1–7), confession (vv.8–10), petition for pardon (vv.11–14), and doxology (v.15). Ryle’s APOT notes highlight the biblical mercy-formula behind vv.6–7, the real-captivity force of the iron-chain imagery in v.10, and the prayer’s sustained dependence on scriptural penitential language. Davila’s OTP discussion is commonly cited for the prayer’s Second Temple penitential theology, especially the paradox that repentance is “appointed” for sinners and the central metaphor “I bend the knee of my heart.”

sources.charles_apot: R. H. Charles (ed.), The Apocrypha and Pseudepigrapha of the Old Testament in English, vol. 1 (Oxford, 1913), Prayer of Manasses / R. H. Ryle, Internet Archive djvu text.
sources.nets: A New English Translation of the Septuagint, Odes / Prayer of Manasses, PDF at ccat.sas.upenn.edu.
sources.nrsv: Bible Gateway NRSVA Prayer of Manasseh page.
sources.commentary: Verse notes paraphrase Ryle 1913 plus brief fact-level paraphrase of Davila in Charlesworth OTP 2 (1985) on the prayer’s penitential structure and rhetoric.

1.charles_apot: O Lord Almighty, God of our fathers, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and of their righteous seed;
1.nets: O Lord Almighty, God of our fathers, of Abraam and Isaak and Iakob and of their righteous offspring,
1.nrsv: O Lord Almighty, God of our ancestors, of Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and of their righteous offspring;
1.verse_commentary: The opening formula is covenantal and patriarchal; Ryle treats it as intentionally biblical and liturgical in register.

2.charles_apot: Who hast made heaven and earth, with all the ornament thereof;
2.nets: you who made the sky and the earth together with all their order,
2.nrsv: you who made heaven and earth with all their order;
2.verse_commentary: The prayer opens with creator language, not immediately with confession, giving repentance a theological frame.

3.charles_apot: Who hast bound the sea by the word of Thy commandment;
3.nets: you who shackled the sea by the word of your ordinance,
3.nrsv: who shackled the sea by your word of command, who confined the deep and sealed it with your terrible and glorious name;
3.verse_commentary: Sea-binding imagery keeps the invocation in the realm of cosmic sovereignty.

4.charles_apot: Who hast shut up the deep, and sealed it by Thy terrible and glorious Name;
4.nets: you who shut up the deep and sealed it with your awesome and notable name,
4.nrsv: at whom all things shudder, and tremble before your power,
4.verse_commentary: Sealing the deep with the divine name intensifies awe before any confession begins.

5.charles_apot: Whom all men fear, and tremble before Thy power; for the majesty of Thy glory cannot be borne, and Thine angry threatening towards sinners is intolerable.
5.nets: at whom all things shudder and tremble from before your power,
5.nrsv: for your glorious splendour cannot be borne, and the wrath of your threat to sinners is unendurable;
5.verse_commentary: Ryle stresses the unbearable majesty/anger language as the backdrop against which mercy will become astonishing.

6.charles_apot: Infinite and unsearchable is Thy merciful promise;
6.nets: both immeasurable and inscrutable is the mercy of your promise,
6.nrsv: yet immeasurable and unsearchable is your promised mercy,
6.verse_commentary: The prayer pivots here from power to mercy; commentators regularly treat this as the transition into its main theological theme.

7.charles_apot: For Thou art the Lord Most High, of great compassion, long-suffering and abundant in mercy, and repentest Thee for the evils of men.
7.nets: you are Lord Most High, compassionate, slow to anger and abounding in mercy.
7.nrsv: for you are the Lord Most High, of great compassion, long-suffering, and very merciful, and you relent at human suffering. O Lord, according to your…
7.verse_commentary: Ryle notes echoes of Exodus 34 and Joel 2; Davila likewise treats the mercy-formula as programmatic for the whole prayer.

8.charles_apot: Thou, therefore, O Lord, that art the God of the righteous, hast not appointed repentance unto the righteous … but Thou hast appointed repentance unto me that am a sinner.
8.nets: you did not appoint repentance for righteous ones … but you appointed repentance for me, the sinner,
8.nrsv: Therefore you, O Lord, God of the righteous, have not appointed repentance for the righteous, for Abraham and Isaac and Jacob, who did not sin…
8.verse_commentary: The contrast between the righteous patriarchs and the speaker’s repentance is intentionally sharp and paradoxical.

9.charles_apot: For the sins I have sinned are more in number than the sands of the sea … and I am not worthy to behold and see the height of heaven by reason of the multitude of mine iniquities.
9.nets: I sinned beyond the number of the sand of the sea; my acts of lawlessness multiplied, O Lord.
9.nrsv: For the sins I have committed are more in number than the sand of the sea; my transgressions are multiplied, O Lord, they are multiplied! I am not…
9.verse_commentary: Confession is piled up lexically: sins, transgressions, iniquities; the rhetoric is cumulative, not redundant.

10.charles_apot: Bowed down with many an iron chain, so that I cannot lift up mine head by reason of my sins; neither have I any respite.
10.nets: Bent down I am by many a fetter of iron … and I find no relief.
10.nrsv: I am weighted down with many an iron fetter, so that I am rejected because of my sins, and I have no relief; for I have provoked your wrath and have…
10.verse_commentary: Ryle argues the iron-chain imagery refers to real captivity/punishment, not merely a metaphor for sin.

11.charles_apot: And now I bow the knee of mine heart, beseeching Thee of Thy gracious goodness.
11.nets: And now I bend my heart’s knee, begging for kindness from you.
11.nrsv: And now I bend the knee of my heart, imploring you for your kindness.
11.verse_commentary: “I bend the knee of my heart” is the prayer’s signature metaphor; Davila and later commentators treat it as the emotional center of the petition.

12.charles_apot: I have sinned, O Lord, I have sinned, and I acknowledge mine iniquities.
12.nets: I have sinned, O Lord; I have sinned, and my lawless acts I know.
12.nrsv: I have sinned, O Lord, I have sinned, and I acknowledge my transgressions.
12.verse_commentary: The double confession is formal, emphatic, and penitential, not merely informational.

13.charles_apot: But, I humbly beseech Thee, forgive me, O Lord, forgive me, and destroy me not with mine iniquities.
13.nets: I plead, begging you; relieve me, O Lord; relieve me!
13.nrsv: I earnestly implore you, forgive me, O Lord, forgive me! Do not destroy me with my transgressions! Do not be angry with me for ever or store up evil…
13.verse_commentary: The repeated plea for forgiveness marks the prayer’s climactic supplication.

14.charles_apot: Neither, in Thy continual anger against me, lay up evil in store for me … For Thou, O Lord, art the God of them that repent; and in me Thou wilt show forth Thy goodness.
14.nets: in me show your goodness, because, though unworthy, you will save me.
14.nrsv: and in me you will manifest your goodness; for, unworthy as I am, you will save me according to your great mercy,
14.verse_commentary: Commentators note that the line about not storing up evil/condemning to the depths focuses on withheld punishment, not merely inward guilt.

15.charles_apot: And I will praise Thee for ever all the days of my life: for all the host of heaven doth sing Thy praise, and Thine is the glory for ever and ever. Amen.
15.nets: I will praise you always throughout the days of my life … and yours is the glory forever. Amen.
15.nrsv: and I will praise you continually all the days of my life. For all the host of heaven sings your praise, and yours is the glory for ever. Amen.
15.verse_commentary: The doxology closes the movement from cosmic invocation to personal confession and back to heavenly praise.
